title: Ignore policy

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Ignore policy; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Git Reference Documentation; current stable accessed 2026-07; section applicable to Ignore policy
sources.claim: Ignore rules suppress untracked-path reporting and accidental addition; they do not remove files that Git already tracks.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Ignore rules suppress untracked-path reporting and accidental addition; they do not remove files that Git already tracks.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Adding .env to an ignore file prevents a new local .env from appearing as untracked, but a previously committed .env remains tracked. Your goal is to create and test a project-appropriate ignore file. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Filesystem and terminal fluency; Repository and working tree. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: Ignore rules suppress untracked-path reporting and accidental addition; they do not remove files that Git already tracks. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Rule order, negation, directory patterns, global excludes, nested ignore files, and path anchoring affect which untracked paths match. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: Adding a secret file to .gitignore erases it from existing commits and remote history.

includes: Ignore generated, machine-local and secret files without hiding required source or removing already tracked files by mistake.
Create and test a project-appropriate ignore file.
excludes: Rule order, negation, directory patterns, global excludes, nested ignore files, and path anchoring affect which untracked paths match.

outcomes: Create and test a project-appropriate ignore file.

examples.title: Ignore policy: direct case
examples.situation: Adding .env to an ignore file prevents a new local .env from appearing as untracked, but a previously committed .env remains tracked.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: Adding .env to an ignore file prevents a new local .env from appearing as untracked, but a previously committed .env remains tracked.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Ignore affects future untracked-path selection only; stop tracking current content separately and rotate or purge exposed secrets using an explicit incident process.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Ignore affects future untracked-path selection only; stop tracking current content separately and rotate or purge exposed secrets using an explicit incident process.

examples.title: Ignore policy: boundary case
examples.situation: A broad build/ rule ignores generated output while a documented negation keeps one required fixture within that tree visible.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: A broad build/ rule ignores generated output while a documented negation keeps one required fixture within that tree visible.
Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Rule order, negation, directory patterns, global excludes, nested ignore files, and path anchoring affect which untracked paths match.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: Adding a secret file to .gitignore erases it from existing commits and remote history.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Create and test a project-appropriate ignore file.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Adding a secret file to .gitignore erases it from existing commits and remote history.
misconceptions.correction: Ignore affects future untracked-path selection only; stop tracking current content separately and rotate or purge exposed secrets using an explicit incident process.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: Adding a secret file to .gitignore erases it from existing commits and remote history.

analogy.example: A photo studio can model Ignore policy: the set is the working tree, a selected proof is the index, and an archived photograph is a commit snapshot.
usefulFor: Separating working changes, staged content, committed snapshots, and repository metadata
limitations: Git stores content-addressed objects and references rather than physical photographs or one linear album.

recap.summary: You should now be able to create and test a project-appropriate ignore file. Keep this boundary in view: Rule order, negation, directory patterns, global excludes, nested ignore files, and path anchoring affect which untracked paths match.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Ignore policy without using the optional analogy and include one observable consequence.
Why is this statement incorrect: Adding a secret file to .gitignore erases it from existing commits and remote history.
Apply the model to this case from memory: A broad build/ rule ignores generated output while a documented negation keeps one required fixture within that tree visible.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “Selective staging and first commit”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
